reviews.author: Shakeel Butt
reviews.summary:
Reviewer noted that reverting d9b3ce8769e3 might not be the best solution as it was landed after a change to reduce global lock contention, which could make the flush cost lower and thus render the ratelimiting unnecessary.

Reviewer noted that even with per-subsystem locks, flushing is still expensive due to kernel traversing the cgroup update tree for each CPU on the system, and suggested moving specific stats from rstat to a separate mechanism where updates can be more expensive but reads can be cheap.

Reviewer expressed preference for a more sophisticated rate limiting approach over reverting the patch, suggesting that a general solution should be explored first
reviews.raw_body:
Hi Jan & Joanne,

On Wed, Jul 15, 2026 at 06:28:16PM -0700, Joanne Koong wrote:

---

Unfortunetely global lock was just part of the cost and even per-subsystem
lock reduces contention across subsystems but flushing is still expensive as the
kernel may have to traverse the cgroup update tree for each cpu on the system.

CCing Yosry who is looking into similar issue but for zswap stats. Maybe a
general solution for such specific stats is to move them from rstat to a
separate mechanism where update can be a bit more expensive but the read side
can be cheap.

---

First let's see if the general solution is doable and simple otherwise we can
explore use-case specific solutions. In extreme case revert is an options as
well but I would prefer a more sophisticated ratelimiting (that Jan suggested)
than a revert.

reviews.author: Joanne Koong (author)
reviews.summary: Author acknowledged that reverting the original fix might be sufficient, but will wait for Yosry's work on a more general solution before making any changes.
reviews.raw_body:
Sounds good. I'll keep an eye out for the work Yosry's doing on this
and look into hooking it up to writeback if/when his general solution
becomes available.

Thanks,
Joanne

2026-07-16.patch_summary: This patch reverts a previous change that disabled default bdi (block device interface) strictlimiting, which was intended to prevent the number of dirty pages from exceeding a certain limit. However, it's been found that this change may not be necessary anymore due to subsequent changes in the cgroup locking mechanism, and reverting it resolves performance issues on xfs file systems. The patch suggests that the flush rate limiting might no longer be needed with the current locking scheme, making this revert a simple fix.
